markets.content: Markets got hit hard Friday. The S&P dropped 1.33% and the Nasdaq fell 1.59% — the primary driver is the Middle East war. Oil is at $92/barrel (Brent) after surging ~30% in a week as the Strait of Hormuz is effectively shut down. That's ~20% of global oil and LNG supply choked off. Gas at the pump is already up to $3.32/gal nationally and analysts say $3.50 is coming fast. Rising energy costs are reigniting inflation fears and kicking rate-cut bets down the road. Safe-haven plays (gold, silver) caught a bid. Your energy-adjacent names (UUUU, NLR, XLU) are worth watching in this environment — this is exactly the macro scenario that moves them.

stories.headline: Oil surges past $90 as Middle East war disrupts global supply
stories.summary: WTI crude breached $90/barrel for the first time since 2023. Brent hit $92, up 30% on the week. Strait of Hormuz disruptions are preventing tankers from loading in Iraq, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia.

stories.headline: Oil prices surge to highest since 2023 as Middle East war rages
stories.summary: Stocks fell globally on Friday as rising energy costs reignited inflation fears, dimming prospects for near-term Fed rate cuts.

world.content: The Middle East is the only story that matters right now. Everything else is downstream of this.

stories.headline: US-Israel war with Iran enters Day 8 — over 1,200 dead, Strait of Hormuz effectively closed
stories.summary: Coordinated US and Israeli strikes have killed 1,230+ in Iran, 200+ in Lebanon. Iran is retaliating with ballistic missiles against Israel and Gulf states. Trump approved a new $151M arms sale to Israel and said he won't negotiate without Iran's 'unconditional surrender.' The US claims 3,000+ targets struck in the first week. 'March for Peace' protests are scheduled across the US today.

stories.headline: Oil at $92 — energy shock threatens global inflation recovery
stories.summary: A 10% rise in crude adds ~30bps to inflation and shaves 15bps off GDP growth. Central banks may have to delay rate cuts. Analysts estimate traders are paying a $14/barrel war premium right now.

stories.headline: Nepal election: RSP heading for landslide victory
stories.summary: Nepal's Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) is leading significantly in election results, signaling a major political shift in the Himalayan nation.

sports.content: Tatum is back and looked real. 15/12/7 in 27 minutes — not a full-throttle effort, but the reads were clean, the movement looked fluid, and the Celtics won by 20. That's everything you needed to see. Watch the next 3-4 games to see how the burst develops as the minutes restriction comes off.
upcoming: NBA: OKC Thunder vs. Golden State Warriors — tonight
NBA: Detroit Pistons vs. Brooklyn Nets — tonight
World Baseball Classic: Taiwan vs. Cuba

stories.headline: Tatum returns — 15/12/7 in 27 min, Celtics rout Mavs 120-100
stories.summary: Jayson Tatum's season debut came less than 10 months after a torn right Achilles. Jaylen Brown added 24. Cooper Flagg had 16 for the Mavs. JT was on a minutes restriction — the real test starts next week.

stories.headline: Luka drops 44 in 3 quarters as Lakers crush Indiana
stories.summary: Doncic hit 7 threes, grabbed 9 boards, and sat out the fourth. Lakers win 128-117.

entertainment.content: March is a big month for film. Three movies worth knowing about.

stories.headline: Project Hail Mary — Ryan Gosling's sci-fi epic drops March 20
stories.summary: Directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller. Gosling plays astronaut Ryland Grace on a solo mission to save Earth. Based on Andy Weir's novel — same guy who wrote The Martian. Two weeks out.

stories.headline: Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man — Cillian Murphy back as Tommy Shelby
stories.summary: The theatrical film continuation of the BBC series. Murphy reprises his role during WWII. One of the most anticipated March releases globally.

stories.headline: Pixar's 'Hoppers' out now — girl transfers consciousness into a robotic beaver
stories.summary: Released March 6. Pixar exploring consciousness and animal connection. Early reviews are strong.

marketing.content: AI is eating the ad industry from the inside out — and the brands that don't adapt operationally are already behind.

stories.headline: Meta launches Manus AI inside Ads Manager — automated campaign planning is here
stories.summary: Meta's Manus AI agent handles campaign planning and competitor analysis directly in Ads Manager. Google is rolling out AI-optimized non-skippable YouTube CTV ads. OpenAI has partnered with Criteo to serve ads inside ChatGPT. GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) is becoming a real discipline as half of US consumers now use TikTok as a search engine.

stories.headline: Netflix + Amazon partner on commerce signals for performance ad targeting
stories.summary: Netflix is leveraging Amazon's 'trillions' of commerce signals via a new Conversion API, transforming streaming from a brand-awareness play into a measurable performance channel. This is a major shift for entertainment marketing.

rabbit_hole.title: Play-Doh Was a Wallpaper Cleaner (And Wrigley Started Selling Soap)
rabbit_hole.content:
In the 1930s, a soap company called Kutol Products invented a pliable putty to clean soot off wallpaper. It worked well — until coal heating declined after WWII and nobody needed their wallpaper cleaned anymore. The company was heading toward bankruptcy when a nursery school teacher suggested repurposing the compound as a children's toy. They added colors, renamed it Play-Doh, and launched in 1956. It became one of the most iconic toys in American history.

This is not a unique story. Wrigley's chewing gum was originally a free premium thrown in with baking powder — customers cared more about the gum than the actual product, so Wrigley pivoted the entire company. Nokia started as a Finnish paper mill in 1865. Samsung began as a trading company selling dried fish and noodles in 1938. The company you think of as a phone company or electronics giant started by shipping groceries.

The pattern: the most valuable thing a company builds is often the byproduct of what they were actually trying to do. Play-Doh wasn't an invention — it was a pivot from failure. The skill isn't just building something; it's recognizing when the accidental output is worth more than the original plan. In a world where everyone is building AI tools and features, this is worth sitting with: what are you accidentally making?
